name: Grammar: Parts of Speech, Subject-Verb Concord, and Tenses

sourceRefs.label: 2025_q1 page 39 Q87-89 (parts of speech; concord with collective/uncountable-looking nouns; tense form-filling, with answer key)
2025_q page 137 Q166, 168 (general sentence-correctness; past perfect continuous with duration, with answer key)

researchNotes: Opens Part C Unit I (Grammar, 25 questions — the largest single track in Part C, now that Unit VII is fully done). 5 real hits found in a focused, targeted search, immediately confirming Grammar is well-represented in the question bank. This topic deliberately groups three closely-related basics (identifying parts of speech, subject-verb agreement/concord, and tense forms) since each individually was too thin for its own topic, but together form a natural 'sentence fundamentals' unit. A second topic (Sentence Patterns and Non-Finite Verbs) covers the remaining Grammar evidence found in this same research session. Expansion pass (Batch 12): added collective-noun agreement, preposition-pairing rules, correlative conjunctions with the nearer-subject agreement rule, the silent-h/vowel-sound article rule, and simple-present-vs-continuous — with tricks/tips added throughout since grammar rules are genuine memorizable shortcuts.

reading.intro: Covers identifying parts of speech, subject-verb agreement (concord) — including tricky nouns that look plural but take singular verbs — and correct tense usage, especially the past perfect continuous for actions with duration. First topic of the new Part C Units I-VI track, replacing Unit VII now that all 59 literary works are done.

qa.question: Real exam: In "He makes rather a fuss about ordinary things," what part of speech is 'rather'?
qa.answer: An ADVERB (modifying the degree of 'a fuss') — confirmed by a real exam question, distinguishing it from being mistaken for an adjective.

qa.question: Real exam: Which sentence is grammatically correct regarding subject-verb agreement — 'His wages is Rs. 1,000 a day', 'Measles are a disease', or 'Politics has divided the country'?
qa.answer: 'Politics has divided the country' is correct. Certain nouns ending in 's' (Politics, Measles, Mathematics, Physics, News) are treated as SINGULAR despite their plural-looking form, taking a singular verb ('has', not 'have'). By contrast, 'wages' is always plural (takes 'are', not 'is'), and 'Measles' (a disease name) takes a singular verb ('is', not 'are') — both of the other two options were marked incorrect for getting this backwards.

qa.question: Real exam: What is the grammatically correct form of the idiom about time, and what error does a common variant contain?
qa.answer: 'Time and tide WAIT for no man' is correct. A real exam flagged 'Time and tide WAITS for no man' as containing a subject-verb agreement ERROR — the compound subject 'Time and tide' takes a plural verb.

qa.question: Real exam: Fill in the blanks with the correct verb forms — 'She ___ tired because she ___ the house yesterday' (using 'is/was' and a form of 'clean').
qa.answer: 'She WAS tired because she HAD BEEN CLEANING the house yesterday' — simple PAST tense for the main state, and PAST PERFECT CONTINUOUS for the earlier ongoing action that caused it, confirmed by a real exam question.

qa.question: Real exam: Which tense is used for an action that had been ongoing for a specific duration up to a point in the past — e.g., 'At that time, she ___ a book for three months'?
qa.answer: PAST PERFECT CONTINUOUS: 'At that time, she HAD BEEN WRITING a book for three months' — confirmed correct by a real exam question, which flagged the simple past ('was writing') as the error to be corrected when duration up to a past point is specified.

qa.question: Real exam: Among 'He is junior than me', 'He is a MLA', 'She is the cleverest girl in the class', and 'This is a best poem of Tagore' — which is the ONLY fully correct sentence, and what's wrong with each of the others?
qa.answer: 'She is the cleverest girl in the class' is correct (superlative 'cleverest' correctly takes 'the'). Errors in the others: 'junior THAN me' should be 'junior TO me' (comparison with 'junior'/'senior' uses 'to', not 'than'); 'a MLA' should be 'AN MLA' (the article depends on the SOUND of the next word — 'MLA' is pronounced starting with a vowel sound 'em', so it needs 'an'); 'a best poem' should be 'THE best poem' (superlatives take 'the', not 'a/an').

qa.question: What is the general rule for collective nouns (team, family, government, committee, jury) taking a singular or plural verb?
qa.answer: SINGULAR when the group acts as ONE unit ('The team IS playing well today'); PLURAL when members act as SEPARATE individuals ('The team ARE arguing among themselves about strategy'). Most TNPSC questions default to the singular/unit sense unless the sentence clearly signals individual disagreement.
qa.tip: Quick test: if you can mentally insert 'as a single group' and the sentence still makes sense, use singular. If you'd naturally say 'members of the team', use plural.

qa.question: What are common preposition-pairing errors TNPSC tests — e.g., 'married ___', 'different ___', 'arrived ___ the station'?
qa.answer: 'Married TO' (not 'married with'); 'different FROM' (not 'different than', though 'than' is common informally); 'arrived AT' a station/small place, but 'arrived IN' a city/country ('arrived to' is always wrong). Other common pairs: 'depend ON', 'succeed IN', 'good AT', 'angry WITH' a person but 'angry AT' a situation.

qa.question: What are the standard correlative conjunction pairs tested in TNPSC grammar, and what verb-agreement rule applies to 'either...or'/'neither...nor'?
qa.answer: Standard pairs: 'either...or', 'neither...nor', 'not only...but also', 'both...and', 'whether...or'. For 'either...or' and 'neither...nor' joining two subjects, the VERB AGREES WITH THE NEARER SUBJECT — e.g., 'Neither the teacher nor the students WERE present' (verb agrees with 'students', the nearer subject).

qa.question: What is the article rule for words starting with silent 'h' or a vowel LETTER but consonant SOUND (e.g., 'honour', 'university', 'European')?
qa.answer: 'AN hour' (silent 'h', so it sounds like it starts with a vowel), but 'A university' and 'A European' (both start with a vowel letter 'u'/'e' but a consonant 'y'-sound) — the rule always follows the SOUND, never the letter, same principle as 'an MLA'.
qa.tip: Say the word aloud — if it starts with a consonant SOUND, use 'a'; if it starts with a vowel SOUND, use 'an', regardless of the actual first letter.

qa.question: How does TNPSC typically test simple present vs. present continuous for habitual vs. ongoing actions?
qa.answer: Simple present for habits/general truths ('He WALKS to school every day'; 'The sun RISES in the east'); present continuous for actions happening right now or temporary ongoing situations ('He IS WALKING to school right now'; 'She IS STAYING with her aunt this week').

questions.prompt: In "He makes rather a fuss about ordinary things," the word 'rather' is a/an:
Which of the following sentences is grammatically correct?
options: The wages of sin is death
His wages is Rs. 1,000 a day
questions.explanation: 'Politics has divided the country' — Politics takes a singular verb.
questions.trick: Memorize this fixed list of 's'-ending nouns that are always SINGULAR: Politics, Mathematics, Physics, News, Measles, Mumps — 'wages' is the opposite trap, always plural.

questions.prompt: Identify the sentence with an error in subject-verb agreement:
options: The team has been working hard
Time and tide waits for no man
questions.explanation: Should be 'Time and tide WAIT for no man'.

questions.prompt: Fill in the blanks correctly: "She ___ tired because she ___ the house yesterday."
Find the error and pick the correct sentence: "At that time, she was writing a book for three months."
options: At that time, she have writing a book for three months
At that time, she had writing a book for three months
At that time, she had been writing a book for three months
At that time, she has writing a book for three months
questions.explanation: had been writing — past perfect continuous.

questions.prompt: Which of the following sentences is fully correct?
options: This is a best poem of Tagore
She is the cleverest girl in the class
questions.explanation: 'She is the cleverest girl in the class.'

questions.prompt: Which correction fixes "He is junior than me"?
options: He is more junior than me

questions.prompt: Why does 'MLA' take the article 'an' rather than 'a' (e.g., "He is an MLA")?
options: MLA starts with a consonant letter but a vowel SOUND ('em')
It is a proper noun exception
There is no rule — both are acceptable
questions.explanation: Articles depend on sound, not the letter — 'MLA' is pronounced starting with a vowel sound.

questions.prompt: Which sentence correctly uses the collective noun 'team'?
options: The team are playing well today (as one unit)
The team is playing well today
The team be playing well today
questions.explanation: 'The team IS playing well today' — singular when acting as one unit.
questions.trick: If the group acts as ONE unit, use singular; if members act individually/separately, use plural.

questions.prompt: Choose the correct preposition: "He is married ___ my sister."
questions.trick: Common fixed pairs to memorize: married TO, different FROM, depend ON, good AT, angry WITH (a person).

questions.prompt: Choose the correct preposition: "This answer is ___ from what I expected."
"Neither the teacher nor the students ___ present." Choose the correct verb.
questions.explanation: 'were' — with either/or and neither/nor, the verb agrees with the NEARER subject ('students', plural).
questions.trick: In either/or and neither/nor constructions, always match the verb to whichever subject is CLOSEST to the verb, ignore the other one.

questions.prompt: Choose the correct article: "She waited for ___ hour before the train arrived."
questions.explanation: 'AN hour' — the 'h' in 'hour' is silent, so it starts with a vowel sound.

questions.prompt: Which sentence correctly distinguishes a habitual action from an action happening right now?
options: He is walking to school every day
He walks to school every day; he is walking right now
He walk to school every day
questions.explanation: Simple present for habits, present continuous for now.
